diseases.label: Head and Neck Cancer
diseases.snomed_term: Malignant tumor of head and neck

chemotherapy_drugs.label: Tamoxifen
activity_precautions: Stay active to reduce clot risk

profiles.label: Breast cancer, ECOG 0-1, no severe fatigue

contraindicates.reason: Fall risk due to sensory loss

adapts.adaptation: Reduce to 20 min, add rest breaks
Use compression sleeve, light weights only
Use supportive footwear, flat surfaces only
Hold onto support, seated exercises preferred
Reduce duration to 20 min, monitor fatigue
Use walking aid if needed, flat surfaces only
Slow pace, take breaks as needed
Schedule in evening, avoid stimulating activities
Monitor blood glucose before/after, carry snack

guidelines.source: ACSM Exercise Guidelines for Cancer Survivors
guidelines.content: For cancer survivors, 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise is recommended. For those with functional limitations, any amount of physical activity is beneficial.
For patients with moderate fatigue, light activity such as walking or yoga is recommended. For severe fatigue, rest is prioritized with gentle breathing exercises when tolerated.

guidelines.source: ESMO Breast Cancer Clinical Practice Guidelines
guidelines.content: Physical activity during and after treatment improves quality of life. Aquatic exercises are particularly beneficial for patients with lymphedema risk.
For patients with hypertension, avoid high-intensity exercise. Moderate walking is safe and beneficial for blood pressure management.
Patients with low white cell counts should avoid public pools and crowded gyms. Outdoor activities in open spaces are preferred.
Patients with bone metastases should avoid high-impact activities and heavy resistance training. Low-impact activities like walking and aquatic therapy are safer alternatives.
Balance training is recommended for patients with peripheral neuropathy to reduce fall risk. Avoid activities requiring fine motor control of feet.
Patients with treatment-related cardiotoxicity should undergo cardiac assessment before exercise. Light to moderate activities with heart rate monitoring are recommended.

guidelines.source: IPOS Standards of Care for Distress Management
guidelines.content: Mind-body interventions including yoga, tai chi, and relaxation therapy are effective for managing anxiety and depression in cancer patients.
Patients with hemoglobin below 10 g/dL should limit exercise intensity. Those with severe anemia (Hgb <8) should avoid all but gentle activities.

guidelines.source: WHO Guidelines on Physical Activity and Sedentary Behaviour
guidelines.content: Adults with chronic conditions including cancer should aim for 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week. Some physical activity is better than none.

guidelines.source: ASCO Exercise Recommendation for Cancer Patients
guidelines.content: Exercise during cancer treatment is safe and beneficial. Supervised exercise programs are recommended when available. Individualized programs based on treatment type are optimal.

guidelines.source: ESC Position Paper on Cancer Treatments and Cardiovascular Toxicity
guidelines.content: Patients receiving anthracyclines (doxorubicin) should have cardiac monitoring. Exercise is beneficial but intensity should be moderated. Avoid high-intensity intervals.
Patients on taxanes (paclitaxel, docetaxel) should be monitored for peripheral neuropathy. Balance training and supportive footwear are essential. Avoid uneven surfaces.
Exercise may enhance immunotherapy efficacy. Moderate activity is encouraged unless autoimmune adverse events occur. Monitor for unusual fatigue or symptoms.
Patients with brain tumors may have balance, coordination, or cognitive challenges. Supervised exercise with fall prevention measures is recommended. Avoid activities with seizure risk.

guidelines.source: IMWG Guidelines for Multiple Myeloma and Bone Health
guidelines.content: Multiple myeloma patients with bone involvement should avoid high-impact activities. Low-impact exercises like swimming, walking, and gentle stretching are preferred.

guidelines.source: AHNS Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancer Rehabilitation
guidelines.content: Head and neck cancer patients may benefit from jaw exercises, range-of-motion activities, and respiratory exercises. Monitor for lymphedema in neck and face.
Patients with liver cancer and cirrhosis should exercise cautiously. Low-intensity activities are preferred. Monitor for ascites and encephalopathy symptoms.

guidelines.source: Bone Health in Hormone Therapy for Cancer
guidelines.content: Patients on hormone therapy (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors) should engage in weight-bearing exercise to protect bone density. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation recommended.
